relevantAnatomy: Transverse articulations between proximal and distal rows are reinforced by palmar and dorsal intercarpal ligaments and carpal collateral ligaments (Miller p.7)

Median nerve passes through the carpal tunnel between FDS and flexor carpi radialis (FCR) to supply the radial lumbricals, thenar musculature via a deep recurrent branch, and sensation to the volar aspect of thumb, index, long, and radial half of the ring fingers (Miller p.20)
At the wrist, the radial artery reaches the dorsum of the carpus by passing between the FCR and the abductor pollicis longus (APL) and EPB tendons (snuffbox) (Miller p.23)
APL frequently has multiple tendon slips, which should be addressed during release for de Quervain tenosynovitis) (Miller p.15)
De Quervain tenosynovitis Extensor carpi radialis lon- gus (ECRL), brevis (Miller p.15)
Ligament of Testut (radioscapholunate ligament) functions as a neurovascular conduit (Miller p.7)
Palmar (volar plate), collateral, and deep transverse metacarpal ligaments (Miller p.9)

Radius and ulna connected by oblique interosseous membrane/ligament (disrupted in Essex-Lopresti injury), which transmits axial load from radius at wrist to ulna at elbow location and function WRIST AND HAND I Carpal bones (Miller p.7)
structuresAtRisk: Risks: FCU stripped subperiosteally to protect ulnar nerve and artery I Surgical approaches to the wrist and hand (Miller p.33)
Protect and retract these tendons to allow access to the distal radius and the dorsal radiocarpal joint (Miller p.33)

relevantAnatomy: Supraspinatus tendon is exposed, which allows for repairs of the rotator cuff (Miller p.26)
Rotator cuff function: depress and stabilize the humeral head against the glenoid (Miller p.107)
force-couple larger shoulder muscles to maintain humeral head center of rotation (Miller p.107)
Coracoacromial ligament contributes to anterosuperior stability in rotator cuff deficiency and should be preserved with irreparable cuff tears to prevent anterosuperior escape (Miller p.2)
Innervation of all rotator cuff muscles is derived I Brachial plexus injury (Miller p.17)
Dynamic stabilizers: rotator cuff and biceps tendon (Miller p.2)
scapulothoracic mechanics contribute to stability (Miller p.2)

Coracoid: attachments to coracoid include coracoacromial ligament, coracoclavicular ligaments (conoid [medial] and trapezoid [lateral]), conjoined tendon (coracobrachialis and short head of biceps), and pectoralis minor (Miller p.2)

Extensor tendinitis (intersection syndrome) Extensor pollicis longus (Miller p.15.0)
Dissect supinator from radius to expose elbow capsule and anterior radius (Miller p.28.0)

Medi Radial nerve Musculocutaneous nerve Median nerve Median Ulnar nerve Ulnar Triceps Biceps Brachialis Brachioradialis Pronator Palmaris (From Miller MD et al: Orthopaedic surgical approaches, ed 2, Philadelphia, 2014, Saun (Miller p.28.0)
structuresAtRisk: Risks: FCU stripped subperiosteally to protect ulnar nerve and artery I Surgical approaches to the wrist and hand (Miller p.33.0)
Dissection through the TCL must be performed carefully to avoid injury to median nerve or its motor (Miller p.33.0)
Using an incision within the anatomic snuffbox (first and third dorsal wrist compartments) helps protect the superficial radial nerve and radial artery (deep) (Miller p.33.0)
